sentences.text: Good afternoon.
My name is F-I-N-K-E-L-S-T-E-I-N, Z-A-L-M-A-N.
Zalman.
Yes.
No.
No.
Zalman.
Wait, wait a minute.
Wait a minute.
I'm a little foolish.
[LAUGHS]
In July 18.
In Lodz.
Lodz, L-O-D-Z.
In Poland.
I just in July 18, I finished 70 years.
What do you?
Do you need the, the street?
We lived on the fifth floor in a big building.
Apartment building.
No, no.
It was a big, a big building, six floors high.
Oh, just on one floor, you had about 10 families living.
Well, near the end, we were eight kids, and father and mother.
When I was young, about 8-9 years or whatever, he was a tool and die maker, an engineer.
He finished engineering.
And he had a helper in the beginning.
But later on, he used to work for the Polish government.
He used to make like this for rifles, tool, parts.
At that time, they couldn't make it.
They used buy the government from-- from Czechoslovakia.
So he took it over.
And he, he, he made the most of it for the government.
And he made a good living out of it.
But then his sister, she was in a-- in a better area, you know, richer people and things, so she had a restaurant, a big restaurant.
So she have, why you have to work, with that and this and that, and support the Polish government.
You know, they kill Jews, pogroms they made here and there in towns.
So he said, yeah, good.
I'll go with you.
So this what-- That's what he did.
He used to do.
No, no.
We were living.
But downstairs was a little room downstairs, that he paid extra for it.
And he used it.
Downstairs, in the back yard.
It was non-Jewish too, but mostly Jewish.
This was like, it was already-- you know, Hitler, Hitler made the ghetto, in this place, the blocks.
Because the majority was Jewish, and it's concentrated.
Yeah.
We were living in it.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Oh, a lot.
You had-- you had a lot of business, a lot of manufacturers, textile, shoes, clothing, anything, stores, bakery.
Was a life like heaven.
Heaven.
Was Christian store, but not too many, but were Christians.
My mother had a good Christian, she used to come light the Shabbos, to cook a cup of coffee or whatever.
So she would light it.
And she was like a sister to mine, mine, mine mother.
Yeah.
I had one boy.
One boy was friendly.
No.
The only problems what my older brothers had, my brother with the owner.
He was a German.
You had a lot of Volksdeutsche in, in, in Poland, in Lodz particularly, a lot of Volksdeutsche.
When the war broke out, he used to-- making with the light, with the mirror, making with the mirror to the airplanes.
Signal them, I don't what he signaled, but he gave them some signs.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
They used to come.
I said-- not too many.
Because across the street was already antisemits.
There was a-- was a store there.
And she had a few kids, three or four kids.
And those kids, they didn't like Jews.
A matter of fact, when the Germans came in, her right away, swastika see every day-- with-- you know--
Arm band.
Oh, a lot.
A lot.
I used to go to a school that I had to-- I had to walk a long way, a long way, 15, 20 minutes walk, good walk, running.
And you pass by on the sidewalk, this was Christians, no Jews then this area.
So they're always-- they're hiding in the-- in the-- in the-- in the building.
And we used to go in a group, because we knew.
So we used to go 10 kids, eight kids, you know, together.
And then when we start seeing something already when you pass they hide inside, in the building.
You know, they used to lock the doors at night, 11 o'clock they lock.
Because there are drunks and things.
So everybody-- the, the guy who cleaned the sidewalk, this part, he used to lock.
If somebody worked, after 11:00, he ring the bell.
And they used to pay him some money, so he came out from the house and opened the door.
So we knew already where they hide.
So we-- once we, we pass the bad part, we start running already.
So then they jumped out, and they're after us.
So they used to-- we used to fight.
We used to get-- they used to have sticks and things.
But this is what happened, oh, a lot of-- Did anybody ever protect you?
No protection, no policemen.
They always come-- they came always when was already finished.
When it was finished, then they showed up, what happened here.
I finished in Poland three, four classes, three or four.
It was a, a, a, a Jewish school.
Public school, a public school.
Jewish support, philanthropists, Jewish people.
Only Jewish kids.
They teach Hebrew.
They teach general things, history; general things, everything.
Everything what a normal school--
Jewish people.
My teacher was a woman.
And I was always-- a funny thing, I don't know if I can say this-- but it was a funny thing.
I-- she was very tall.
I think-- I don't know today.
She looks to me she was like all the way to the ceiling.
But, you know, as a kid you cannot judge that right.
But anyway, so I, I always lift up the hand to answer.
You know, she used to write something on the-- on the wall, the thing.
It was not-- it was a wall painted.
It didn't even have what to write.
Just a wall, and they paint this black.
And then we used to write on it.
So I used to lift up, and want to answer.
You sit.
You sit there.
You just answered before.
Let somebody answer.
So sometimes, again, you know something new came up.
She walked over, lift me up by the ears, and you stay in the corner.
[LAUGHS] Because I don't listen.
Because you answer when, when I told you, OK.
A nice lady.
In what language were the classes held?
In Polish, just Polish.
Because they didn't allow it.
Yes.
No, most, most was in, in Polish schools.
I had a rabbi home, coming.
We had a rabbi in our building, Rabbi Worm.
And he always sit outside on the-- on, on the steps, and talking to kids stories.
You know?
Chumash, Gemara, you know, Tanakh.
No, no, no.
They were going to cheders and things.
No, no.
They didn't have nothing to do with the public schools.
No.
We were not Hasidic.
But we were kosher and believed in the religion, believed in Shabbos, believed in yontif, you know.
Classic, the best you can see a person dressed.
He was such a-- my mother the same thing, such a clean-- you could eat from the floor.
Excellent.
Yeah, yeah.
I don't know if the pants were different than non-Jews.
But he was really dressed up, especially when he already start-- he left this, his work, and he went to work with the-- with the sister, in this huge restaurant.
And this-- the best area was in Lodz, the best, this is.
This is-- This was Piotrkowska, [NON-ENGLISH], all the way-- you know, Nowomiejska, you know, all those streets they were rich.
[NON-ENGLISH], she had the restaurant.
It's-- I can't even put my tongue--
They-- like the rich people.
It's like over here, Fifth Avenue.
It was there.
I don't know.
Big restaurant.
Oh, no.
Pants, a shirt, like me, you know, nice.
He had a hat, he had a hat, not yarmulke.
He wear the hat.
A kapelusz.
You know, a kapelusz, like you wear a--
A hat, yeah.
A kapelusz, is like Polish.
No.
He had it like the regular big hat.
No, she was just-- like hair.
No, no.
Her hair.
She was young.
Excellent.
Candlelight, fish of Shabbos, [NON-ENGLISH] every Shabbos, every Shabbos, [NON-ENGLISH] benched.
Mother benched over the candles.
And my father used to make a [NON-ENGLISH], normal.
But they, they were not fanatics.
No, no.
We didn't sing.
But sometimes the kids start singing a song from school, Yiddish song.
I have at home written.
No.
I have the words, but I cannot put it together.
I have a little booklet with me that I brought from the camp too.
But yeah, we used to-- a little.
About holidays, about freedom, about happiness, loving, you know, kissing each other, songs.
Flowers in it.
In Yiddish.
Yeah, kids together.
Sometimes father used to say, you sing it.
Let me see if you know it.
We had in school songs.
And those songs, we used to bring it home.
And my father used to bring from restaurant, from over there, from other singers, you know, they used to sing [NON-ENGLISH], little short pieces, [NON-ENGLISH], used to come, a chazzen, you know.
Used to come there, and [NON-ENGLISH] and this and that.
[NON-ENGLISH] he used to sing.
So he used to write it down, bring it home for Saturday, Friday night.
No.
I have it in my head.
But I-- I-- you know, it's, it's over 50 years, 55 years.
You know, I think if would be normal situations, I think you would remember everything.
Because you repeated.
Yiddish, Yiddish.
Every holiday.
We used to-- we have this-- we had a-- we had a sukkah [NON-ENGLISH].
A joint, a community sukkah.
Yeah, we had a--
Downstairs in, in, in the-- in the-- in the back yard, in the back.
So we used to big [NON-ENGLISH].
So I don't remember, six families, five families together.
Every time, we couldn't leave it.
No, no.
You, you-- fresh, fresh.
Because we're lucky that every time we went down that it's still there.
Because some-- some kids-- not kids.
Even grownups, they broke it, you know.
They broke the wall, broke this down.
They ripped the, the--
The s'chach on top, you know.
So--
No, nobody.
They were afraid.
Are you kidding?
Yeah.
This we eat.
Yeah, we ate there, sure, even it was broken.
So we just quick put it back up, a little bit, and we showed the light and everything was there.
Passover.
[LAUGHS]
They bought me new pants.
I got new pants, a new jacket, new shoes, new socks.
I know the young kids, they got-- the big brothers already-- I don't know what happened there.
But they were dressed not bad.
My mother.
No help.
Some cook, some cook.
Anything she cooked was out of this world.
And my daughter-- you're going to meet my daughter, as sweetest person you ever can see, and she's number two from my mother.
She give away everything what she has, didn't care.
[SIGHS] In our house, in this building was not Hasidic Jews.
I lived [NON-ENGLISH] 9, this was the street and the number of the building.
In [NON-ENGLISH] 7, one building on the side there, was a wooden building.
Over there you had 90% Hasidic, 90%.
And a matter of fact, I still have a, a friend in Israel, although I saw him-- say, what year was it-- that I did a lot for this man in the camp.
Well, I'm, I'm, I'm going to get off track in the camp.
You needed a partner in the camps.
If he finds something to eat, he shared with me.
I, I organized something, they used to say organizing something.
If I found something to eat, I didn't eat myself.
I shared with him.
And he came from [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
Now he came from a, a religious home.
His father is a shochet and a rabbi.
I used to go even to him to learn.
And oh, religious, payos, you know.
And the [NON-ENGLISH] payos.
And we were kept together.
Not bad, not bad at all.
It was very good.
Because we all were like, even if you don't have the payos, but you eat.
They used to say-- but they knew too what you're eating, even the whole building.
They know each other, you know?
So they knew what you're eating, who is your father, what's your father doing, how you feel.
But everybody if not religious, they ate kosher food, they had good butchers.
Yeah, Dror Habonim.
Dror Habonim.
This is already the later years, late, I started.
They accept me already, you know, to continue.
Completely excellent.
Completely, no complaints.
They didn't complain, the other side didn't complain.
They just don't join the same group as you.
You know, he sits all day and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father had to go to work.
So it's a-- it was a different-- today is the same thing here.
You know, I helped out a few years ago, when I came.
I was working on my own.
You know, I was building, and I was fixing anything to houses.
In Monticello area.
I, I build a few bungalows, you know.
So I, I made-- I made-- I made a good living.
I made money.
I came here as a professional.
I didn't try this to do.
I didn't want it.
I knew this to do too, you know, woodwork.
A matter of fact, I have a lot of the stuff from wood homemade.
And--
For Hasidim too, yes.
Yes.
I built for [NON-ENGLISH], and it changed.
You want to an addition or something, I did.
It's OK people.
I have never had any problem--
No, no.
Not in Lodz.
Not in towns.
I didn't have a bar mitzvah.
Because the war broke out at that time.
They have a person.
He's a rabbi-like.
He's the rabbi.
He sits all the time in the kehillah, in the shul.
And people coming, and he takes a, a, a, a, a, a [NON-ENGLISH] or takes a gemara out.
And he explains this to people sitting around.
And he translates from the Hebrew, whatever it's written in the Bible, the way it's written, he translates this in Yiddish.
The kehillah.
Sure.
The rabbonim are poor.
The machers is, is the people that they support the shul.
They hold the shul.
But rabbonim, normally they were poor people.
Poor, because they just sit and study.
They used to have separate.
Sometime attached to the shul.
A lot of them had attached.
Richer, they had separate, completely away from it, away from it.
But most was together, like separate.
And they had-- they had in the shuls, poor shuls, they has classes in the shuls.
After the davening--
They used to put together the-- the bank and this together, no chairs, you know, and sit there.
No.
He was not involved.
Not involved.
And the Bundist movement, was that--
Maybe, yes.
I don't know.
But I think not.
Maybe yes, you know.
He doesn't give me, a kid, all the-- all the details.
You know, what you know about it?
Yeah, yeah.
Well, it's-- if Friday night, we had to be everybody together having the dinner, and candlelight.
So everybody.
Now if was time, we used to go downstairs in front of the building, and face talk a little bit, and that's it.
Next day, across the street from us was a place.
But we didn't do the Shabbos.
We had to ride bicycles, especially for a few [NON-ENGLISH].
So they had bicycles.
And you used to go up, and you know, like kids going around there.
We used to be this.
In wintertime, used to skating there, skates, and now a street.
We used to go-- I had another friend that the father used to-- very nice people, they were.
He used to sell horses, buy and sell it.
Once a week was a big market from farmers coming, not far, in the same block but the end was a huge place.
It was a market, and you could [INAUDIBLE] from the best you want.
You know, and it was a good place, it was nice.
And the area, you used to walk in the street.
Summer used to go, it was a place where it was a manufacturer of bricks, used to make bricks.
And, and over there was a huge lake.
So we used to go a little swimming there, and this around it.
All, all-- and even the lake was where Christians were going, and where the [NON-ENGLISH] going.
This side [NON-ENGLISH], and this side goyim.
Didn't go there.
We were afraid.
They had a better spot, you know.
Was sand there.
We had mud to go in.
[LAUGHS]
But we were OK.
Once you start swimming, you don't care what in-- is on the bottom, but till you got to be a little deeper that you can start swimming, so you went till here in the mud, you know.
So always this way that you were the secondary, you know.
And you learned this. you learned this.
Don't, don't, don't.
